title: Social movements and equal protection
description: Test your skills in this quiz on social movements and equal protection.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best illustrates the concept of civil rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The rights of individuals against discrimination based on their race, sex, or other demographic characteristics
The rights of individuals against unjust governmental interference
The rights of individuals to life, liberty, and property
The rights of individuals to ask government for redress of grievances

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would likely be considered an unconstitutional use of state power under the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Florida passes a law denying legal counsel to suspected terrorists
A state law criminalizes intermarriage between white and black citizens
Idaho refuses to recognize drivers’ licenses issued in states that do not strictly enforce immigration laws
Michigan passes a law requiring individuals to be 21 or older to marry

assessment_items.question_content:
**Based on previous rulings, which of the following scenarios would most likely violate the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A state law criminalizes intermarriage between white and black citizens
Police use a fake warrant to enter a woman’s home and then arrest her for possessing obscene materials
A school district allows students of two religions, but not students from a third, to use auditoriums for group prayer and meditation
A city ordinance prevents protests from civil rights groups

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following actions may Congress take to advance civil rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Pass a law requiring that all schools receiving federal funding comply with desegregation efforts
Issue an executive order desegregating the military
Declare Voter ID restrictions unconstitutional
Strike down state laws banning racial quotas in university admissions

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Who can say that the legislature of Alabama which set up that state's segregation laws was democratically elected? Throughout Alabama all sorts of devious methods are used to prevent Negroes from becoming registered voters, and there are some counties in which, even though Negroes constitute a majority of the population, not a single Negro is registered. Can any law enacted under such circumstances be considered democratically structured?”

> -Excerpt from King, Martin Luther Jr., “Letter from the Birmingham Jail” in _Why We Can’t Wait_, ed. Martin Luther King, Jr., 77-100, 1963.

**The “devious methods” mentioned in this passage refer to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Party-line voting
Segregation
Demographics
Structural barriers

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of civil liberties and civil rights?**

 | Civil liberties | Civil rights
- | - | -
**A** | Freedom from racial discrimination | Right to vote 
**B** | Freedom from unreasonable search and seizure  | Freedom of religion
**C** | Freedom of assembly | Freedom from gender discrimination
**D** | Right to privacy | Right to a fair court trial

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines civil rights? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Rights that guarantee individuals freedom from discrimination at both the state and federal levels of government
Freedoms that give people the right to refuse to obey laws as a peaceful form of political protest
Rights that are given to the states to protect them from the federal government superseding their power
Freedoms that protect citizens, opinions, and property against government abuse of power

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an example of a social movement motivated by the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The women’s rights movement
The Progressive movement
Occupy Wall Street
Abolitionism

assessment_items.question_content:
>“An unjust law is a code that a majority inflicts on a minority that is not binding on itself. This is difference made legal. On the other hand, a just law is a code that a majority compels a minority to follow, and that it is willing to follow itself. This is sameness made legal.”

>-Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., *Letter from a Birmingham Jail*, 1963. 

**Which of the following constitutional provisions guarantees that “sameness [is] made legal” in King’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment
The establishment clause of the First Amendment
The faithful execution clause of Article II
The full faith and credit clause of Article IV

assessment_items.question_content:
>“An unjust law is a code that a majority inflicts on a minority that is not binding on itself. This is difference made legal. On the other hand, a just law is a code that a majority compels a minority to follow, and that it is willing to follow itself. This is sameness made legal.”

-Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., *Letter from a Birmingham Jail*, 1963. 

**Supporters of King’s view that just laws treat the majority and minority the same could point to which of the following Supreme Court cases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka* (1954)
*Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District* (1969)
*McDonald v. Chicago* (2010)
*McCulloch v. Maryland* (1819)

assessment_items.question_content:
>“No union is more profound than marriage, for it embodies the highest ideals of love, fidelity, devotion, sacrifice, and family . . . It would misunderstand these men and women to say they disrespect the idea of marriage. Their plea is that they do respect it, respect it so deeply that they seek to find its fulfillment for themselves. Their hope is not to be condemned to live in loneliness, excluded from one of civilization's oldest institutions. They ask for equal dignity in the eyes of the law. The Constitution grants them that right.”

>-Justice Anthony Kennedy, majority opinion in *Obergefell v. Hodges* (2015)

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Marriage is a fundamental right under the Fourteenth Amendment’s equal protection and due process clauses
A new constitutional amendment should protect the fundamental right of marriage
The institution of marriage is more important than the Constitution
Individuals should be able to declare their love for each other openly based on the First Amendment’s protection of freedom of speech

assessment_items.question_content:
>Who can say that the legislature of Alabama which set up that state's segregation laws was democratically elected? Throughout Alabama all sorts of devious methods are used to prevent Negroes from becoming registered voters, and there are some counties in which, even though Negroes constitute a majority of the population, not a single Negro is registered. Can any law enacted under such circumstances be considered democratically structured?

> -Excerpt from Martin Luther King's “Letter from the Birmingham Jail,” 1963.

**Which statement best explains King's argument about democratic participation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Through devious methods, the majority of Alabama state legislators are able to undermine federal laws outlawing segregation.
The federal government’s undemocratic control of the Alabama state legislature is responsible for preventing African Americans from registering to vote.
Segregation laws and the disenfranchisement of African Americans are unjust if they are not supported by the state legislature.
A law affecting African Americans is unjust if they were denied the right to vote and thus had no part in creating the law.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best explains why the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment has been an effective tool for social movements seeking to expand civil rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It offers a constitutional foundation for legal challenges against discriminatory state laws.
It ensures that interest groups have the right to petition the government and request financial support.
It affirms that all citizens have the right to due process under the law.
It prevents Congress from passing laws that infringe on state authority.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the women’s rights movement of the 1960s and 1970s take a different approach that the earlier Civil Rights Movement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It used court cases along with efforts to add a new amendment to the Constitution.
It avoided working with Congress and focused primarily on passing legislation at the state level.
It focused on changing public opinion and culture instead of using arguments about the Constitution.
It focused on civil disobedience and did not attempt to take legal action in the courts.

assessment_items.question_content:
In the decades following the women's rights movement of the 1960s and 1970s, the federal government took steps to increase gender equality in education. One result was a dramatic increase in girls’ participation in school sports, rising from under 300,000 athletes in the early 1970s to over 3 million athletes by 2020.

**Which government action most directly contributed to this increase?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A federal law prohibited sex-based discrimination in education programs receiving public funds.
A constitutional amendment was ratified guaranteeing equal access to public education for all.
A Supreme Court decision upheld the federal government’s authority to enforce gender equality in education programs.
State legislatures passed laws mandating gender quotas in school athletics participation.
